summary: Gruber analyzes NPD data showing Apple captured 66% of the U.S. retail PC market for machines costing $1,000 or more in Q1 2008. He argues that overall PC market share has always been a misleading metric for Apple because it includes commodity machines Apple never intended to compete against. He notes Apple's share in this premium segment surged from 18% in January 2006 to 66% in just two years. He pushes back on Paul Thurrott's framing that this merely proves Macs are expensive, arguing instead that it demonstrates Apple's growth is far more impressive than raw unit numbers suggest.
key_points: Apple held 66% of the U.S. brick-and-mortar retail market for PCs priced $1,000+ in Q1 2008, up from 18% just two years earlier
Overall PC market share is a misleading metric because it lumps premium computers with commodity machines Apple doesn't try to sell
Retail sales are a meaningful signal of individual consumer preference since most people choose their home computer but not their work one
Framing the data as 'Macs are expensive' misses the point — Apple's growth in the premium segment indicates a dramatic consumer shift toward Macs
Apple's share would likely be even more dominant at higher price tiers like $1,500+ and $2,000+
money_quotes: The idea that Apple now sells two-thirds of retail computers costing $1000 or more is simply stunning.

A simplistic 'overall PC market share' number has never been a good metric for gauging the Mac's success because the 'overall PC market' includes millions of commodity-level low-end machines that Apple neither tries nor wants to sell.

It's like counting everything from golf carts to tractor trailers as a single category, thus making the 'overall market share' look worse than it is for a company that only makes actual passenger cars.
Apple's share of the overall profits in the PC hardware industry would be a far better measure of success than their unit sales share.
Apple's recent market share growth is far more impressive than unit sale numbers indicate.
So from 18 to 66 percent in two years — in what had seemed until very recently a relatively old and stable market.
People who care about computers, at least insofar as 'caring' means 'spending over $1000', are switching to Macs.
key_insight: Raw market share numbers systematically understate Apple's success because the metric that matters isn't units sold but dominance of the premium segment where consumers are voting with real money — and by that measure, Apple's growth from 18% to 66% in two years was a seismic shift.
